name: celery
summary: Distributed task queue for async and scheduled background jobs in Python.

story.problem: Web requests need to return fast. Sending emails, resizing images, calling slow APIs, or running ML inference inside a request handler blocks the server and degrades UX. You need a way to hand off work to a separate process and respond immediately.
story.mental_model: Celery is like a post office. Your web app drops tasks into a mailbox (broker — usually Redis or RabbitMQ). Worker processes pick them up, execute them, and optionally drop results in a result backend. The web app never waits.
story.when_to_use: Sending emails or notifications, processing uploads, running scheduled cron-style jobs, chaining multi-step workflows, fan-out parallel processing.
story.when_not_to_use: Simple scripts that don't serve web traffic — just use asyncio or threading. If you need sub-millisecond latency, Celery's overhead is too high. For tiny projects, consider RQ (simpler) or FastAPI BackgroundTasks.

alternatives.name: RQ
alternatives.reason: Simpler Redis-backed queue, no broker config, great for smaller projects.

alternatives.name: Dramatiq
alternatives.reason: More reliable at-least-once semantics, simpler API, growing popularity.

alternatives.name: arq
alternatives.reason: Async-first, asyncio-native, minimal overhead. Good for FastAPI/aiohttp stacks.

alternatives.name: Huey
alternatives.reason: Lightweight, single-file, Redis-backed. Easy to embed in small Django/Flask apps.

nodes.label: celery
nodes.summary: Top-level package. Import Celery app class.

nodes.label: Celery(app)
nodes.summary: App factory: `app = Celery('myapp', broker='redis://...')`. Holds config, task registry, beat scheduler.

nodes.label: @app.task
nodes.summary: Turn any function into a Celery task. Adds .delay(), .apply_async(), .s() methods.

nodes.label: .delay(*args)
nodes.summary: Shortcut to enqueue a task: `add.delay(1, 2)`. Returns AsyncResult.

nodes.label: .apply_async(args, kwargs, countdown, eta)
nodes.summary: Full task dispatch with options: delay countdown, ETA, expiry, queue routing.

nodes.label: AsyncResult
nodes.summary: Handle to a running/completed task. .get() blocks for result, .state, .ready(), .failed().

nodes.label: chain / chord / group
nodes.summary: Canvas primitives: chain(a, b) pipes output, group([a, b]) runs in parallel, chord waits for group.

nodes.label: Celery Beat (scheduler)
nodes.summary: Periodic task scheduler. Define CELERYBEAT_SCHEDULE or use django-celery-beat for DB-backed schedules.

nodes.label: Broker (Redis/RabbitMQ)
nodes.summary: Message transport. Redis is easiest; RabbitMQ is more durable. Set via broker_url.

nodes.label: Worker process
nodes.summary: `celery -A myapp worker`. Executes tasks. Concurrency via prefork, eventlet, or gevent.

tasks.title: Define and run your first background task

steps.label: Install
steps.explanation: Also install a broker — Redis is the simplest choice.

steps.label: Create app
steps.code:
from celery import Celery
app = Celery('tasks', broker='redis://localhost:6379/0')

steps.label: Define task

tasks.title: Schedule a periodic task with Celery Beat

steps.label: Config
Define tasks
Chain
steps.code:
from celery import chain
result = chain(fetch_data.s(url), process.s(), save.s()).delay()
print(result.get())
